perturbation.original_text: Each part y shall hold in trust and confi dence and prot ect and treat as strictly secret and confidential at all times the Inform ation received from the other party, and in respect of su ch Inform ation, each party agrees that it: (a) shall not directly o r indirectly d isclose, verbally, in writin g or in any other form, or allow the Inform ation to be di sclosed to any other person, fi rm or com pany; (b) shall keep secret and shall not use, m anipulate or exploit the Information, except for the Purpose and then only in strict accordan ce with the Purpose and the terms of this Agreem ent; (c) shall restrict disclosure of th e Inform ation to such of i ts employees or consul tants or subcont ractors who need t o know for t he Purpose and shal l procure t hat such persons observe ful ly the provi sions of this Ag reem ent as if th ey were th emselves parties to it; (d) shall not copy , store or di stribute any Inform ation or al low any Inform ation to be copi ed, st ored or distributed except for such copi es, st orage or di stribution as are st rictly requi red for t he Purpose; NDA Page 2 of 2 (e) shall protect the com mercial inte rest of the disclosing party and sh all take the sam e precautions to protect the Inform ation as a careful and prudent person woul d be expect ed to employ for i ts own business and confi dential inform ation and, i n doing so, shal l use reasonabl e care t o protect the Inform ation from unaut horised use, harm , expl oitation, m anipulation, m odification, interference, misuse, m isappropri ation, copy or di sclosure what soever, except as specifica lly au thorised by the disclosing part y in writing; (f) shall keep all In formation that is electronically stored in a tangible form segregat ed from all of its own propert y and i n a safe and secure envi ronm ent and protect and keep safe all such Inform ation from any loss, harm , theft, unaut horised use, t ampering, sabot age, unaut horised dupl ication, dest ruction, damage or interference whatsoever (g) shall, upon dem and, inform the disclosing part y of the location of t he Inform ation and t he measures that it h as taken to preserv e its co nfidentiality; (h) shall retu rn to the disclosing party all In formation, copi es and t angible manifestations t hereof, i n any form whatsoever, an d delete all In formation from all retriev al system s and databases o r destroy the same immediately upon t he direction of t he disclosing part y, and furni sh to the disclosing part y, immediately upon i ts request , a cert ificate of a seni or offi cer or di rector attesting to such ret urn, deletion or dest ruction, as t he case m ay be; (i) shall treat co mmunicatio ns with the other party as co nfidential an d utilise reaso nable care in maintaining the confidentiality o f communicatio ns even if th ey do not include Information: and (j) shall be liable to the other party fo r compliance with the term s and conditions of this Ag reem ent, and shall fully indem nify the other part y and hol d it harmless from dam ages (i ncluding lawyers’ fees and disbursem ents) incurred as a result of a breach of this Agreem ent.
perturbation.changed_text: Each part y shall hold in trust and confi dence and prot ect and treat as strictly secret and confidential at all times the Inform ation received from the other party, and in respect of su ch Inform ation, each party agrees that it: (a) may disclose Inform ation to any affiliate companies without prior consent; (b) shall keep secret and shall not use, m anipulate or exploit the Information, except for the Purpose and then only in strict accordan ce with the Purpose and the terms of this Agreem ent; (c) shall restrict disclosure of th e Inform ation to such of i ts employees or consul tants or subcont ractors who need t o know for t he Purpose and shal l procure t hat such persons observe ful ly the provi sions of this Ag reem ent as if th ey were th emselves parties to it; (d) shall not copy , store or di stribute any Inform ation or al low any Inform ation to be copi ed, st ored or distributed except for such copi es, st orage or di stribution as are st rictly requi red for t he Purpose; (i) shall treat co mmunicatio ns with the other party as co nfidential an d utilise reaso nable care in maintaining the confidentiality o f communicatio ns even if th ey do not include Information: and (j) shall be liable to the other party fo r compliance with the term s and conditions of this Ag reem ent, and shall fully indem nify the other part y and hol d it harmless from dam ages (i ncluding lawyers’ fees and disbursem ents) incurred as a result of a breach of this Agreem ent.
perturbation.explanation: Section 4 initially requires that neither party disclose Information. By changing clause (a), now a party *may* disclose Information to affiliate companies without consent. This change directly contradicts the initial statement that the party will hold the information in confidence and not disclose it to any person or company as stated in the first line of section 4, creating an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.justification: The original text states that neither party can disclose information. The changed text allows a party to disclose information to affiliate companies without prior consent. This creates a direct conflict because the initial requirement of strict confidentiality is now undermined by the allowance of disclosure to affiliates. This aligns with the definition of 'Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction' as a clause is placed in multiple locations with conflicting implications, leading to uncertainty about the confidentiality obligations. The first line of the section (the contradicted text) says the party will hold the information in confidence and not disclose it to any person or company. The added clause states that they *may* disclose Information to any affiliate companies without prior consent.

perturbation.original_text: The obl igations cont ained herei n shall not appl y to Inform ation whi ch: (a) at the tim e of disclosure, is with in the public domain or which, after d isclosure, becomes rightfully available to the public, other than by a breach of this Agreem ent; (b) was independently deve loped or acquired by the receiving party, other than by a breach of this Agreem ent; (c) was rightfully obtained by the receiving party from a third party without restrictions in respect to disclosure or use; (d) the receiving party is by law com pelled to disc lose, provided that the r eceiving party has forthwith notified the disclosing party of an y such com pelled disclosure when the receiving party first becom es aware o f any such legal obligation to disclose: or (e) is explicitly ap proved for release b y written authorisatio n of the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: The obl igations cont ained herei n shall not appl y to Inform ation whi ch: (a) at the tim e of disclosure, is with in the public domain or which, after d isclosure, becomes rightfully available to the public, other than by a breach of this Agreem ent; (b) was independently deve loped or acquired by the receiving party, other than by a breach of this Agreem ent; (c) was rightfully obtained by the receiving party from a third party without restrictions in respect to disclosure or use; (d) the receiving party is by law com pelled to disc lose, provided that the r eceiving party does not need to notify the disclosing party of an y such com pelled disclosure when the receiving party first becom es aware o f any such legal obligation to disclose: or (e) is explicitly ap proved for release b y written authorisatio n of the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: Section 5(d) originally stated that in cases of legally compelled disclosure, the receiving party *must* notify the disclosing party. This change eliminates the notification requirement. This contradicts the obligation to protect the information's confidentiality outlined in Section 4, as not notifying prevents the disclosing party from seeking legal recourse to protect its confidential information, thus affecting the enforceability of Section 4.

perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 5(d) removes the requirement for the receiving party to notify the disclosing party of legally compelled disclosures. This directly contradicts Section 4, which mandates strict confidentiality and protection of the disclosing party's information. By removing the notification requirement, the disclosing party is prevented from taking action to protect their confidential information, creating a clear conflict between the obligation to maintain confidentiality and the ability to do so.

perturbation.original_text: The parties’ obligations hereunder shall: (a) be unlimited in territo ry; (b) only expi re ten (10) y ears from the dat e of this Agreem ent or in the case of technical Inform ation at the end of t he com mercial useful ness t o the disclosing part y; and (c) survive the term ination of this Agreem ent for any reason whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties’ obligations hereunder shall: (a) be limited to England; (b) only expi re ten (10) y ears from the dat e of this Agreem ent or in the case of technical Inform ation at the end of t he com mercial useful ness t o the disclosing part y; and (c) not survive the term ination of this Agreem ent for any reason whatsoever.
perturbation.explanation: Section 6 outlines the duration and scope of the NDA. By changing subsections (a) and (c), the confidentiality obligations are now limited to England and *do not* survive termination of the agreement. This contradicts the premise in Section 7, which states that the expiration of the obligations is *without prejudice to any rights* the disclosing party may have under applicable Intellectual Property law, which would inherently imply that some obligations related to protecting that IP *would* need to survive termination to be effective, directly contradiciting the changed section 6
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties acknowledge that the obligations contai ned herein are entirely reasonable and necessary in order to protect th e leg itimate co mmercial, trad e, business an d tech nical in terests o f the disclosing party and t he expi ration of such obl igations i s without prejudi ce to any rights whi ch the disclosing part y may have i n relation to the Inform ation under appl icable Intellectual Propert y law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a direct contradiction. The original text states the obligations are unlimited in territory and survive termination. The changed text limits the obligations to England and states they do NOT survive termination. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which acknowledges the obligations are necessary to protect the disclosing party's interests under applicable Intellectual Property law and that the expiration of obligations is without prejudice to any rights the disclosing party may have. The changed text would undermine the protection of intellectual property after termination and outside of England.

perturbation.original_text: The parties acknowledge that a vi olation by the receiving party of its obligations contained herein m ay cause serio us harm to the disclosing party th at damages may be insufficien t to constitute an adequate remedy. The parties ag ree th at they sh all be entitled , in addition to all o ther rights provided by law o r by this Agreem ent, including m onetary dam ages, t o obtain a perm anent injunct ion or m andatory court order to prevent the violation of t he obl igations cont ained herei n.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties acknowledge that a vi olation by the receiving party of its obligations contained herein m ay cause serio us harm to the disclosing party th at damages may be insufficien t to constitute an adequate remedy. The parties agree that the disclosing party's sole remedy for any breach of this agreement is limited to a refund of any direct costs incurred, and under no circumstances will specific performance, including an injunction or mandatory court order, be available.
perturbation.explanation: Section 8 originally allowed for monetary damages *and* injunctive relief. The change eliminates the possibility of injunctive relief, specifying that the disclosing party is *only* entitled to a refund of direct costs. This creates an in-text contradiction, as the parties previously acknowledged that damages *may be insufficient* and thus agreed to injunctive relief, which is now expressly forbidden. This also directly contradicts Section 13, which states "The part ies shal l do al l such t hings and provi de al l such furt her and ot her assurances as m ay be reasonabl y requi red in order t o give effect to the purpose and i ntent of this Agreem ent." By taking away the possibility of injunctive relief, it makes it impossible to give effect to the intent of the agreement in a violation.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The part ies shal l do al l such t hings and provi de al l such furt her and ot her assurances as m ay be reasonabl y requi red in order t o give effect to the purpose and i ntent of this Agreem ent.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 8 to limit the disclosing party's remedy to a refund of direct costs and explicitly prohibits specific performance, including injunctions. This directly contradicts the original Section 8, which allowed for injunctive relief. It also contradicts the contradicted text, which states that the parties shall do all things necessary to give effect to the purpose and intent of the agreement. By removing the possibility of injunctive relief, the changed text makes it impossible to fully give effect to the agreement's intent in case of a violation where damages are insufficient, as acknowledged in the original text.
